card.title: How Rainbows Appear
card.teaser: Every rainbow is a personal hallucination — made just for your eyes.
card.coverImagePrompt: A vivid double rainbow arching over a wet meadow after a storm, droplets suspended in golden backlight from a low sun, cinematic, painterly

lessons.title: No two people see the same rainbow
lessons.body: Here's a strange truth: if you and a friend stand side by side watching a rainbow, you are not seeing the same rainbow. Each of you is seeing a completely different set of raindrops. Step a meter to the left, and the rainbow steps with you. It's not out there in any fixed place. It's a geometric trick your eyes are catching mid-flight.

visual.imagePrompt: Two people on a hillside seeing slightly different angles of the same rainbow

lessons.title: What each raindrop is doing
lessons.body: White sunlight is a mix of every visible color. When it enters a raindrop, it slows down and bends — a process called refraction. Different colors bend by different amounts: red barely, violet the most. Inside the drop, the light bounces off the back wall, then bends again on the way out. One drop, two bends and a mirror: a tiny prism made of water.

keyTerms.definition: The bending of light as it passes from one material into another
The separation of white light into its component colors

visual.imagePrompt: A magnified raindrop with a colorful spectrum fanning out as light passes through it

lessons.title: The 42-degree secret
lessons.body: Every color leaves the raindrop at a very specific angle from the incoming sunlight — red around 42°, violet around 40°. For you to see a red band, raindrops at exactly 42° from the anti-solar point (the spot directly opposite the sun from your eyes) must be catching light right now. That's why a rainbow is always a circle centered on your shadow's head. You are always at the center of your rainbow.

visual.imagePrompt: A diagram showing the sun behind a viewer casting their shadow, with a rainbow circle centered on the shadow's head

lessons.title: Newton worked it out
lessons.body: In 1666, Isaac Newton passed a beam of sunlight through a glass prism and showed that 'white' light was made of colors. Then he passed it through a *second* prism and recombined it into white again — proving the prism wasn't adding color but separating it. The same physics, scaled up to billions of falling drops, is what paints the arc across a post-storm sky.
lessons.funFact: From a plane or mountaintop, rainbows can appear as complete circles — the ground normally cuts off the lower half.

visual.imagePrompt: A dim candlelit room with a prism splitting sunlight on an old wooden desk

lessons.title: A collaboration, not an object
lessons.body: A rainbow needs three things: water droplets, sunlight from behind you, and an observer at just the right angle. Remove any one, and the rainbow vanishes. It's less an object than a conversation — between a star, a storm, and a pair of eyes. Strange, lovely, and only visible to whoever's standing in exactly the right spot.

quiz.question: Why do you and a friend standing next to each other see different rainbows?
options: The rainbow depends on the viewer's exact angle to the drops
Only one eye can see rainbows at a time
quiz.explanation: A rainbow is defined by the angle between the viewer, the drops, and the sun — each viewer gets their own.

quiz.question: What happens to light inside a raindrop?
options: It refracts, reflects once, and refracts again
It passes through in a straight line
quiz.explanation: Sunlight bends entering the drop, bounces off the back, and bends again leaving — separating into colors.

quiz.question: Where is the center of every rainbow from your perspective?
options: Your own shadow's head (the anti-solar point)
quiz.explanation: Rainbows form a circle centered on the anti-solar point — the spot directly opposite the sun from your eyes.

sources.title: Color and Light in Nature
sources.publisher: David K. Lynch & William Livingston, Cambridge University Press
